allergens: gluten (gram flour may contain traces depending on processing)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Bharwan Rajasthani Mirchi Pakoda is 3–4 pieces, weighing approximately 250 g, providing around 550 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — ICMR-NIN; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central for gram flour and potato fritter analogues
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bharwan Rajasthani Mirchi Pakoda exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for besan (gram flour) pakoda and potato-stuffed fritter analogues, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for gram flour (besan), boiled potato, and deep-fried battered vegetable products. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values are consistent with a deep-fried gram flour battered snack with potato filling at approximately 16 g fat, 14 g carbohydrate, and 4.8 g protein per 100 g. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg as this is a fully plant-based preparation with no animal-derived ingredients.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database analogous food entries for gram flour-based snacks and potato-containing fried foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bharwan Rajasthani Mirchi Pakoda. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous foods from the University of Sydney GI Database: gram flour (besan) pakoda-type snacks and potato-containing deep-fried items. Potato has a GI of approximately 70–85 (high); gram flour batter lowers the composite GI due to its higher protein and fibre content. The composite GI of 65 reflects the blended contribution of potato filling (high GI) and gram flour batter (medium-low GI, ~40–50). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (35 g from 250 g × 14 g/100 g) / 100 = 22.75, rounded to 22.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 320 mg/100 g combined with high fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 pieces and request reduced-salt preparation, as the 320 mg sodium per 100 g combined with the high fat load from deep-frying contributes to elevated blood pressure and arterial stiffness risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium-high glycemic load (22.8 per serving) from potato filling and gram flour batter, compounded by high fat promoting insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish, as the medium GI (65) combined with a high glycemic load per serving (22.8) and 16 g fat per 100 g significantly elevates post-meal blood glucose and promotes insulin resistance over time; an air-fried or baked version with reduced potato filling would substantially lower glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (16 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) from deep-frying in vegetable oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of this dish, as the combination of 16 g total fat, 3.2 g saturated fat, and 0.2 g trans fat per 100 g from deep-frying raises LDL cholesterol and cardiovascular risk; a single 250 g serving delivers approximately 40 g total fat, exceeding the recommended daily saturated fat limit for at-risk individuals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); green chillies and potatoes are nightshade vegetables
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish, as deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and both green chillies and potatoes are nightshade vegetables that may trigger solanine-mediated joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bharwan Rajasthani Mirchi Pakoda is a traditional North Indian deep-fried snack originating from Rajasthan, India, and widely served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent. The dish consists of large, thick-walled green chillies (typically Bhavnagri or similar mild-to-medium heat varieties) that are slit lengthwise and stuffed with a spiced potato mixture (aloo masala) containing boiled mashed potato, cumin, coriander, amchur (dry mango powder), chaat masala, and green chilli. The stuffed chillies are then coated in a thick gram flour (besan) batter seasoned with salt, turmeric, red chilli powder, and ajwain (carom seeds), and deep-fried in hot oil until golden and crispy. The result is a snack with a crunchy, savoury exterior and a soft, spiced potato interior, with the chilli providing a mild to moderate heat depending on the variety used. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 220 kcal, 4.8 g protein, 16 g fat (3.2 g saturated, 0.2 g trans), 14 g carbohydrate, 2.4 g dietary fibre, 320 mg sodium, 24 mg calcium, 1.2 mg iron, and 16 mg vitamin C. The glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 22.8 per standard 250 g serving. Clinically, this dish is high concern for Type 2 Diabetes and cardiovascular disease due to its high fat content, medium-high glycemic load, and trans fat from deep-frying. It is also high concern for rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation and nightshade ingredients. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal. It is commonly served as a street food snack, tea-time accompaniment, or starter at North Indian restaurants.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bharwan Rajasthani Mirchi Pakoda exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN gram flour pakoda and potato-stuffed fritter analogues, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the macronutrient profile is internally consistent and well-supported by the component-level evidence from approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific Rajasthani stuffed chilli fritter preparation are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based preparation containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central analogues for gram flour and potato-based fried snacks. trans_fat_g is 0.2 g per 100 g, reflecting minor trans fat formation from high-temperature deep-frying in vegetable oil, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried battered vegetable products. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and has been retained as the primary nutrient reference. The cultural primary_cuisine field in the input referenced a specific Singapore restaurant name; this has been normalised to North Indian / Rajasthani cuisine. Two duplicate portion entries (both 250 g, 3–4 pieces) were present in the input; consolidated to a single 250 g default serving. Dish is served at North Indian restaurants in Singapore including Bombay Curry And Kebabs and Aryana.
